questionText: Prove that for every integer $n$, $n^3$ is congruent to $0$, $1$ or $8$ modulo $9$.

steps.description: Reduce to the residue of n modulo 3.
steps.explanation: We will see that the residue of $n$ modulo $3$ completely determines $n^3$ modulo $9$, so three cases suffice.
Since $27=9\times3$, the cube is a multiple of $9$, giving residue $0$.
Expanding $(3k+1)^3$ with the binomial gives these four terms.
Each of the first three terms is a multiple of $9$, leaving residue $1$.
Expanding $(3k+2)^3$ gives these terms; note $54$ and $36$ are both multiples of $9$.
The first three terms are multiples of $9$, leaving residue $8$.
The three cases give residues $0$, $1$ and $8$ respectively.
Every integer is congruent to $0$, $1$ or $2$ modulo $3$, so no case is missing.
A cube can only land on $0$, $1$ or $8$ modulo $9$; the other residues are impossible.
The extra factors of $3$ in each expansion become multiples of $9$ when cubed, so only $n\bmod3$ survives.
Equivalently, cubing the representatives $0,1,2$ and reducing modulo $9$ yields the full set of possibilities.
Because the finite list of cases was complete, the claim is proved.
By exhaustion, the cube of any integer is congruent to $0$, $1$ or $8$ modulo $9$.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: For $n=3k,\,3k+1,\,3k+2$ the cubes reduce mod $9$ to $0,\,1,\,8$ respectively (the extra terms are all multiples of $9$). Hence $n^3\equiv0,1$ or $8\pmod9$. $\blacksquare$
